speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 20, 1995. My name is Norma, N-O-R-M-A, Menasche, M-E-N-A-S-C-H-E. The interview is with Khane, K-H-A-N-E, Beltser, B-E-L-T-S-E-R. This is tape one. My name is Norma Menasche. I have the interview with Khan-- Khane Baltser, in Davie, Florida, USA. The date is November 20, 1995. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Khane Baltser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it for me please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-H-A-N-E B-A-L-T-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lithuania, in Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 199-- in 1929, in March 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am 66.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please describe me your life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a big family. I have three brothers. We was two sisters, my father, and my mother. My father have a lot of brothers-- three brothers and two sisters. My mother have two brothers and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Kaunas, in Kovno. In Lithuanian, they mean Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it, the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-V-N-A-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a city was it? How many people, more or less, lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a lot of people. It's difficult to say. But I-- I can't tell exactly how many people was there before--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a big Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish was about 60% in Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would the people work? What kind of profession did the Jew-- Jewish people have?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people-- the professions of people was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some-- they have little stores, fruit stores, from-- I can't explain to you-- different-- different-- a lot of different stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: Businesses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how about your father? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Shleyma Glushak. He was in the business. He have a small business store. And my mother was home. She was never work before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was having the business of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] glass-- glass business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Ethel Glushak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's names-- the oldest brothers was Abraham Glushak. The other brother is-- was Leyba Glushak. And the baby brother was Kolew Glushak.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters-- she was the oldest in us family-- Riva Glushak.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were speaking only in Yiddish. My brothers-- they spoke in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what schools did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Yiddish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- before the war, did you have any experiences with anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little girl. I can't say about anti-Semitism. Because we never talk about this. I mean, it never feel this-- anti-Semitism, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the rise of Hitler before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: About Hitler? I was hearing about they did a lot of pogroms of the Jewish people was in Poland. This was in 1929. And we're like kids. We can't understand in this meantime what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear your parents talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, but not often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if they were making any preparation, if the Germans would invade Russia, Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly I can't say. Because in us family, we was talking only about religious, about happiness. Sometimes I was hearing. But I was a kid. It's-- it's very difficult to-- to say about Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your religious background? Did you-- did you-- did your family participate in-- were part of a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother-- she was very religious. She was a [? Huledet ?] from the Jewish religious-- every Friday to go to the synagogue. On Saturday, she was never cook. She was never do something in the house. This was like a-- a Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your father? Was he-- he religious? Was he-- Did he participate in religious evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody in the family was part of a-- was religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's father-- this was my grandpa-- he was [YIDDISH]. This you can say in-- in Yiddish. In English, I don't know, but [YIDDISH]. I help them [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in your Hebrew school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Hebrew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you-- was your family members of any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know about that. In-- before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war. Were they part of any organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK. How did you find out first about the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out in meantime when the Germans came in, in Lithuania. But before they came, they was started the war in 1941, in the beginning, in-- in-- in June 22. And when-- in June 23, the Lithuania-- the Lithuanian-- we say-- we say call them-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And they were start to killed a lot of Jewish people in Kovno. This was-- it was a terrible [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before the German invaded Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before they came to Lithuania. This was a couple weeks before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans came in, they was-- talked to the Lithuanian [NON-ENGLISH], because we will open a ghetto. And then we will do us things what we need to do to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have to move to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My whole family moved to the ghetto-- my father, my mother, my three brothers. My older sister-- she was married in this meantime. She was separated her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were your brothers at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was the-- my sister, she was 18. My other brother, the oldest, 16. The other brother, 12-- 14. I was 12. And the youngest brother were 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what were your-- your impressions, when you had to go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was crying. We can't understand what happening. And we can't go outside. And we need that have-- my mother, she can't go to buy something-- food. It was a-- terrible thing. And we can't-- we kids, we can't understand what happening. Why did she-- we have this to-- this-- this experience for us?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got-- got to the ghetto, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a house for us family. In this house-- this was a big house. In the same house it was lives my father's parents and my father's youngest brother, with the wife and kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people lived in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in this house was 11 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a house was it? How many rooms did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have four rooms. My grandmother, she was two rooms. And my father's younger brother-- he have three rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Now what were the conditions in the ghetto in the beginning? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a terrible condition. We did not have what to eat, what to drink. We did not have place where to stay. Because a lot of people-- they-- they did not have place to came in. The Lithuanian, in this place where we was, where the-- the German is, they close-- they have the special place for the Jewish, in ghetto. The people was more than apartment. And somebody is outside, and inside. And we was-- we came in ghetto. We was-- did not have where to live. And we was-- the first two months, we was living in a place where neighbors-- they gave to us a room, where they hold it before food, in this room. But we was happy. It's not outside. It's something. We have something on-- from-- from the raining, from the sun, something to close us, to be in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was like-- in Yiddish, I can say a [YIDDISH], a special place for food. It's not apartment. It's nothing there. Only-- only food was, before. But later on, they did not have nothing. And-- and we was-- stayed there, seven people-- six people-- four kids, my father, and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was stay there about three weeks. And later, the Germans-- they started to experience to killed Jewish people in the ghetto. My father-- he was the first survivor. Like, they was talking, they will-- they need men to go to work. And my father-- he talked to my mother, you know, we need food for the kids. I will go to work. My mother-- she was not too happy. But she was, OK, you can go. And in this day-- this was after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were stay in ghetto three, four months-- three, four weeks. And they take 500 young men. And it was-- they can work. They looking nice. My father was 38. And never we saw my father back. They killed him in this-- we didn't-- nobody knows in what place they killed him and what happened about these 500 men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what date that was, when they-- when they took your father, and the 500 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1941, in the beginning, when we were say in ghetto, after three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage afterwards, without your father? How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was living with my mother and three brothers. In October 28, the Germans-- they do a big-- execute for the Jewish people. In this day, they killed 12,000 Jewish people-- women, kids, oldest men, and young men. And in this action, my mother-- she was, and three brothers-- and two brothers-- they killed in this-- in this day, they killed my mother and two brothers, my two oldest brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they went about getting those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- they have-- in Lithuania, they have special places before, with the jail, for people. And this-- in these places, they do big [NON-ENGLISH]. What is in-- in English [NON-ENGLISH]? Was a-- was a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Holes. They were digging holes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Digging-- they digging holes. And they kill-- some-- they killed it. And they put in the people, like in life-- they don't care. It's for two days, they killed all the people, 12,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- in the holes, that they-- the Germans dug the holes? Or they-- they had other people do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people, they don't know. They tell them to work. They do these, the holes. And about 500 people they killed. They-- together with these people were they help, they do the holes. Later, they take other people. The other people do the holes. They bring again 500 people. And they kill together these people, what they helped to do the holes. And they do this to everybody what they help, and everybody-- every-- they killed all the people, 12,000-- 12,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After, when I find out it was my mother they killed, I go to visiting my sister. She was living about 15 minutes from this place where I was, and my family live. And she can't understand what happening, why did she-- I've been here, where I was when they took mother. I tell them, I don't know. I was in another place in this meantime. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my sister, she left me living together with my sister. In this meantime, my youngest brother is coming. And she can't again understand. She ask him. He tell the same, I don't know. Because they-- they-- when they took the-- the people to the place to killed, sometimes they go-- go do in this side. Go do in this side. And he was going in the side. [INAUDIBLE]. He saw that his mother is not here. He say, I have-- I don't know what-- what happened when I was separate from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1941, in October 28.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you lived with your sister, how long did you stay with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my sister two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- that was also in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in the ghetto. But for the time from the two years, the Germans-- they took-- in every when Jewish holidays, they killed a lot of people-- special in the Jewish holidays they do this to us, to remind us, it's your holidays. And we will kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions in the ghetto during those two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: The condition-- we did not have what to eat. We did not have what to drink. We did not have clothes. We did not have to heat up the buildings, the apartments. We did not have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the non-Jewish population react to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In ghetto, [NON-ENGLISH] organization, partisan people, what they was go fighting with the Germans. And these people-- they help a lot us kids. It was-- we was survived from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Action.

speaker: interviewee
text: --action. And they organization a special school for us to learning Lithuanian language, to go out from ghetto, to be in another place, with Lithuanian people. This was my father's youngest sister. She was in this-- to-- in this organization. She was in-- in this organization. And she helped me a lot. She opened a Lithua-- a school. We was learning only Lithuanian. And it was very difficult to-- to came in in the school. We must do to watching everything-- maybe somebody came from Lithuanian [NON-ENGLISH], from a German, the soldiers. And we must be very careful to came in, in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let's get back to the life in the ghetto. Can you tell me how was the everyday life for the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day the Germans took the-- the young people, and people what they can work-- they took us to different places to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was an airport, a big airport. And they have a lot of things to do there. See, some things they changed. I can't say exactly what they do. But they changed the place. They changed the-- the grounds. They changed everything. And they-- every day they took a lot of people from ghetto to this place. This was about-- they never-- they never took in buses that are in some places. We was walking, the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one hour and a half to walk to the-- to the other place, to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work on that-- on that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was some-- sometimes I was work. But I work for somebody else-- and for my sister, for my sister's friends. And they-- they give-- my-- my sister's friends, they give me something to eat for this day-- a piece of bread, a piece of brother-- of butter, sometimes water, sometimes tea-- like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- kids came, when the Germans saw us, it's small kids. And they can't do hard works. They tell us, OK. This is-- this is 40 people. You must do-- care for them, to bring to them water. And when we was have nothing to do, we helped them. We took the-- the hard [NON-ENGLISH]. And we helped them to-- we slept, everything together, with the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of laws did you have in the ghetto at that time? What kind of laws did the Germans impose on the-- on the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean the walls? They-- we have the yellow sign, five-- this is the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Star. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. The Jewish star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we must to be-- everybody must to have, in the front and in the back-- in the back-- two stars. Because they must to know, and they see you in the face, and when they see you in the back, what this is a Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have a-- how were you guarded by the Germans? How was the ghetto guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean guarded?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was watching so the people wouldn't escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. We have a committed, special, nice people, what they explain to us what to do. Today, we must go-- the Germans today need thousand people to work. Today, the Germans need money. Today, the Germans need jewelry. And we must to give to them five, six pounds jewelry. And if we do not do this, they will kill them, the Jewish people. And everybody give up everything what they have to the place. They have a special place, this, and to give up in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the Jewish police chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who chose those people to be policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was from the organization, the special [NON-ENGLISH] of the Jewish people. We know before [INAUDIBLE], the partisan, the organization, they know before what the Germans like to do with us. And somebody like to help. We can't help everybody. But some-- somebody like to help each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said that it was like a committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that committee? Did they-- did it have a name? They were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't-- I don't know. I don't know. I know the name, this from the partisan. And this is-- this was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did you do to get food? How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes my sister-- she helped me. And in 1942, the Germans opened a special place for kids to work. And we go out from ghetto six o'clock, five o-- six o'clock in the morning to 6 o'clock in the evening. They took us in special places, to-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I can't explain to you. [NON-ENGLISH], to do vegetables.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to plant.

speaker: interviewee
text: To planted vegetables. We was working about six months in the-- in-- in the year. We planted. And later on, when the-- the vegetables, they grow out-- up, we took out from the ground the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Harvest.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the vegetables. And it was very hard work. But we was happy. Because we have something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the-- did you have electricity in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any electricity. We did not have any hot stuffs-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about sanitation? Cleanliness, bathrooms, water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Everybody do what he can to do.
